context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem Set 8 as a whole. Could we start with 8.1? I don’t really know how to even set up the normal modes for that 3×3 bead grid.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple. You've likely seen a 1D beaded string — say, 3 beads on a single string with fixed ends. How did you find the normal modes for that system? What was the key idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of 8.1 and feels lost. I should avoid giving any setup directly. Instead, I'll use a Socratic question to activate their prior knowledge about simpler 1D beaded string problems, which is the natural stepping stone to the 2D case. This guided discovery approach keeps the scaffolding minimal and lets the student make the connection themselves.

exchanges.student: I remember it had something to do with assuming sinusoidal motion and turning it into an eigenvalue problem, but I’m fuzzy on why that works. I also don’t know how that idea carries over when it’s a 2D grid instead of just one line.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're on the right track. Let's sharpen that intuition. For a single bead in the 1D string, what does Newton's second law look like? What forces act on it, and what equation do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition (sinusoidal ansatz, eigenvalue problem) but is fuzzy on the reasoning. I should affirm this and push them to think about why a sinusoidal ansatz works — specifically the equation of motion for a single bead. This is the foundation before extending to 2D. I'll ask a targeted question about the equation of motion for one bead in the 1D case to build the bridge.

exchanges.student: This is where I get mixed up — I know neighboring beads pull on it, but I’m not confident about the signs or whether it becomes something like “difference of neighbors minus itself.” I keep feeling like I need to see the whole pattern before I can write even one bead’s equation.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need the whole pattern yet — just focus on one bead. If a string has tension T and the bead next to it is displaced by some amount, what is the transverse force that string segment exerts on your bead? Think about the small angle approximation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling with writing the equation of motion for a single bead. They're circling around the right idea but lacking confidence. Rather than showing the full pattern, I should ask them to focus on just one bead and think about the geometry of the force from a single string segment. This is a targeted question that breaks the problem into the smallest possible piece without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think it’s proportional to the slope, so maybe something like \(T \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta x}\), but then I stop knowing which \(\Delta z\) goes where. And in the 2D problem I’m not sure if I’m supposed to add horizontal-string contributions and vertical-string contributions separately or if that’s overcomplicating it.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — the force from one string is T·(z_neighbor − z_bead)/d, where d is the spacing. And yes, your instinct is correct: the horizontal and vertical string contributions simply add. So for one interior bead, how many neighbors are pulling on it, and can you try writing Newton's second law for that bead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the force from one string segment as T times the slope (small angle approximation). Now I should confirm this and push them to think about what 'slope' means for one bead specifically — the difference between the bead's displacement and its neighbor's displacement divided by the spacing. Then naturally the 2D extension follows from linearity: horizontal and vertical strings contribute independently. I'll affirm their instinct about adding contributions and ask them to write out the full equation for one bead.

exchanges.student: I think four neighbors, but I’m already worrying about edge beads and fixed boundaries and it’s making me lose the thread. Could you just show me the general equation for bead \((j,k)\) in the grid so I can see the structure first?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the equation — that's your job! But here's the good news: forget the edges for now. For an interior bead (j,k) with four neighbors, you already know each string contributes T·(z_neighbor − z_bead)/d. There are two horizontal neighbors (tension Th) and two vertical neighbors (tension Tv). Can you try writing that sum equal to m·z̈?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give the equation directly, which I must refuse. However, they are showing signs of anxiety about complexity. I should redirect them to the interior bead first, reassure them that boundary conditions come after, and ask them to write the equation themselves using what they just correctly identified: 4 neighbors, two tensions.

exchanges.student:
Maybe
\[
m\ddot z_{jk}= \frac{T_h}{d}(z_{j,k+1}+z_{j,k-1}-2z_{jk})+\frac{T_v}{d}(z_{j+1,k}+z_{j-1,k}-2z_{jk})?
\]
I’m not even sure if I swapped \(j\) and \(k\), and then after this I don’t know how people magically jump to the normal modes.
exchanges.tutor: That equation is correct — well done! The j/k labeling is just a convention, don't worry about it. Now, in the 1D case you mentioned a sinusoidal ansatz. Given that your equation separates into x-direction and y-direction terms, what form of solution might you guess for z_{jk}(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equation of motion — this is excellent progress and deserves clear affirmation. The j/k indexing concern is minor and doesn't affect the physics. Now I should push them toward the normal mode ansatz. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them what they would try as a solution, drawing on their earlier memory of the 1D case (sinusoidal ansatz). This is the natural next Socratic step.

exchanges.student: I guess something like a product, but I don’t really see why I should guess that instead of some big 9-component vector thing. I’m also kind of lost on whether the fixed boundaries mean sines automatically, or if that’s only in the continuous string case.
exchanges.tutor: Both approaches are actually equivalent — the product ansatz just organizes the 9-component eigenvector in a structured way. On your boundary question: in the 1D beaded string with fixed ends, what form did the normal mode amplitudes take at each bead position? Does that form automatically satisfy z=0 at the fixed walls?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising two good questions: why a product ansatz, and whether fixed boundaries force sines in the discrete case. These are both valid and connected. I should affirm the 9-component vector approach is also valid (it's the same thing), and ask them to think about what the boundary conditions (z=0 at fixed ends) imply for the discrete case by analogy with the 1D beaded string they already know.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember discrete sines, like \(\sin(n\pi k/4)\) for 3 beads maybe, but I don’t remember why the 4 shows up. And I’m still not seeing how to turn that into the 2D grid without just getting buried in indices.
exchanges.tutor: Good memory! The 4 comes from the fact that with 3 beads and 2 fixed endpoints, you have 3+1=4 intervals. So the allowed values are sin(nπk/4) for n=1,2,3. Now — if the x-direction and y-direction each independently follow that same logic, what would the product ansatz z_{jk} look like for the 2D grid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly recalls discrete sines and is close to understanding the denominator. I should help them see why the '4' appears by connecting it to the boundary conditions (N+1 beads including fixed ends means denominator is N+1). Then I can push them to extend this to 2D using the product ansatz they already guessed, without giving the full answer.

exchanges.student:
I think it would be something like
\[
z_{jk}(t)=A\,\sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\right)\cos(\omega t+\phi),
\]
but I’m not confident at all about which index goes with horizontal vs vertical, and I’d honestly need help with the next substitution step because that’s where I usually get lost.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — excellent work getting there! The index labeling is just convention. Now substitute your ansatz into the equation of motion. Start small: what does the combination sin(pπ(j+1)/4) + sin(pπ(j−1)/4) − 2sin(pπj/4) simplify to? (Hint: use a trig identity.)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the 2D normal mode ansatz — this is excellent progress. I should affirm this clearly. The index concern is again minor convention. Now the key next step is substituting this ansatz into the equation of motion to find ω. I should not do this substitution for them, but ask them to try it — specifically, what happens when they apply the operator (z_{j,k+1} + z_{j,k-1} - 2z_{jk}) to their ansatz. This is a targeted question that moves directly toward the dispersion relation.

exchanges.student: I know there’s some identity that turns that into a constant times \(\sin(p\pi j/4)\), but I can’t pull it out cleanly. I’m getting overwhelmed by the trig and I kind of just want the general frequency formula before I lose the setup again.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give the formula, but here's the exact identity you need: sin(A+B) + sin(A−B) = 2sin(A)cos(B). Apply it to sin(pπ(j+1)/4) + sin(pπ(j−1)/4) with A = pπj/4 and B = pπ/4. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they are genuinely struggling with a specific trig step and showing frustration. I should give them a concrete nudge on the specific identity (sum-to-product) without doing the algebra for them, keeping it very targeted. The identity sin(A+B)+sin(A-B)=2sin(A)cos(B) is the key tool here.
